Cottrellv .Moody, 12 B. M. , 500. But theprovisionsof theCode concerningattachments
have dispensed with the necessityof an injunctionin such cases. 22 194,237 .
2. The chancellorwill not enjoina defendant from relyingon a defence,the right to
whichhe has acquiredwithoutfraud. Bowman v.Violett,4 Mon. ,350.
IV . ENJOINING WRONGFUL ACTS GENERALLY .
1. Illegaltaxes. The chancellorwillenjoin not only the collectionof taxesunlawfully
assessed(3 Bush , 648 ; 5 Id., 243 ; 12 Id., 233 ; 79 Ky ., 295 ; Norman v . Boaz ,85 Ky .,
557),but the proposedassessmentof unlawfultaxes(84 Ky .,502),or the saleof bonds
payableby means of unlawfultaxation. 9 Bush,247.
2. Trespasson land.
Though ,asa generalrule,thechancellorwillnot enjoincommissionof a singletres-
passon land, itwas heldthathe may do sowhen the trespassisthreatenedby a munici-
pal corporation. 12 B. M. , 615-16 .
But thechancellormay enjoincontinuanceofrepeatedtrespassesthreatenedby a person
who is insolvent(I Bush ,463); and " the fact that redresscan alone be had by a multi-
plicityof actionsatlaw,forsimilarand continuedtrespassesupon the property,presents
stronggrounds in favorof the jurisdictionof a courtof equity." 84 Ky .,256; and see
Peak v .Hayden,3 Bush,125. That suggestionissupportedby thegeneralcurrentof
authorityconcerning multiplicityof actions(High on Injunctions, 63); and itseems clear
thatone threatened trespassby an insolventperson- such as a threatto teardown a house,
may furnishbetterground foran injunctionthanthreatsto cut down twentytreeswithin
twenty days .
According to a dictum in Hillman v .Hurley, 82 Ky ., 629, " the same characterof pos-
sessionof realestateisnecessarytomaintainan injunctiontopreventa trespasstoitasto
supporttrespassquareclausumfregit. Unlessthe plaintiffisin theactualpossessionat
thetimeof the injuryor threatenedinjury,he must failin eitheraction. Thiswas the
ruleof the common law,and itprevailedin thisStateuntilthe passageof the act of
March 10, 1854,by which theowner of realestatewas permittedtosueintrespass,even
ifhe did nothave theactualpossession;" and thecourtproceedstosaythatsaidactwas,
by itstitle,made a partof chapter50 of theRevisedStatutes,and was impliedlyrepealed
by itsomission from the General Statutes.
For said act of 1854 and comments thereon, see note b, ante, page 202. As saidenact-
ment speedilyfollowed the decision in Robertson'sadm'rs v .Rodes, 13 B. M. , 325 ; so the
dictumin Hillmanv.Hurley(utteredinMarch, 1885)was followedby an actof March 13,
1888,which isas follows:
" An Act regulatingactionsfor injuryto real estatein thisCommonwealth .- That the
ownerof any landinthisStatemay maintainthe appropriateactionto recoverdamages

230 [TITLE VIII.PROVISIONAL REMEDIES .
forany trespassor injurycommitted thereon,or to preventor restrainany trespassesor
otherinjurytheretoor thereon,notwithstandingsuchowner may nothave the actualpos-
sessionof the land atthe time of the commission of the trespass."
The only differencebetween those actsconsistsin the fact that the lattercontainsthe
words " or to prevent or restrainany trespassesor otherinjurytheretoor thereon." The
plaintiffsin both Robertson'sadm'rs v .Rodes and Hillman v .Hurley claimed to have con-
structivepossessionof the land; and, for reasonsstatedin note(ante,page 103), con-
cerningtheactof 1854,itseems to me thatthe act of 1888was intendedto applyonly
to personshaving constructivepossession,and not to reversionersor equitableowners;
and,therefore,thatitisproperto considerthe rightsof the latterto an injunction,in-
dependentlyof thestatute.
The court,in Hillmanv.Hurley, citesno decisionin supportof itsdictum; and I feel
confidentthat no such decisioncan be found . Trespass on land being " an outrage on the
possession" (4 J.J. M. , 19),the actionof trespasscouldbe maintainedonlyby one in
actualpossession: but a reversionercould maintaincaseforan injurytothe freehold, and
could maintain trespassor troveragainst a trespasserwho removed trees, & c.; and such
actionscould be maintainedby an owner having constructivepossessionof land(note,
ante,page 103): and itseems,a fortiori,that,independentlyof any statute,a reversioner
orconstructivepossessorof land could maintainan actiontopreventthecuttingof trees,
& c.,by insolventtrespassers; and,yet more clear, ifpossible,thatsuch actioncouldbe
maintainedby an equitableowner of land,who could not maintainan actionof either
trespass,trover,or case; and, consequently,thatthe provisionof the actof 1888,as to
preventive remedies, was unnecessary; or, at any rate, would have been so, but for the
dictum in Hillman v .Hurley. See the next followingnote.
3. Waste .
" The distinctionbetween waste and trespassconsistsin the former being the abuse or
thedestructiveuseof propertyby one who ,whilenot possessedof absolutetitlethereto,
has yeta righttoitslegitimateuse: trespassbeing an injurytopropertyby onewho has
no rightwhatever to itsuse." High on Injunction, & 650.
For decisionsof theCourt of Appeals astoinjunctionsagainstwaste,seeDowning v.

a contingentremainder-man can notmaintainan actionforwastecommitted,he may main-
tainan actionto prevent waste.
The decisionsas to injunctionsagainstwaste seem to be conclusiveagainst the dictum
in Hillmanv.Hurley,82 Ky .,629, astothe necessityof actualpossessioninordertoen-
join trespasses.

(1) An indictment,and not an injunction,istheremedy tosuppressa publicnuisance,

(2) Discomfortcausedby the noiseof machineryor of railroadtrains(10Bush,294;

health(Seifriedv .Hays , 81 Ky ., 377), in which the defendant (a butcher) was restrained

plained of."

(3) " Courts of equitywill grant an injunctionto restraina nuisance, only in cases

(4) " The obstructionsof water courses, the diversionof streams from mills, the back

An act of 1797 (St.Law , 294) declaredthat " any person having both the legaltitleto,

full force." Kincaid v .McGowan ,9 Ky . L. R. , 987. Moreover , itseems clearthat such

(d) INSTRUCTIONS .
I. CHARGING THE JURY AS TO FACTS .
The courthas no rightto charge the jury on the facts, as isdone in England : the jury
are to decide the factsunbiased by the court or any judicialsuggestion. 7 B. M. , 658 .
II . GENERAL RULES AS TO INSTRUCTIONS .
I. Duty of courtas togiving instructions.
It isnot the duty of the court to instructthe jury as to points of law unlessa motion
be made forthatpurpose(Hardin,535), though the courtmay do so (7 J.J. M. , 197);
and ifan improperinstructionbe askedfor,thecourtmay overruleitwithoutgivingany
instructionon thepoint. 1 Bibb, 159; 7J.J.M. , 197. But,though not bound todo so,
the court may modify an instructionto meet the law of the case (4 Dana ,424 ; 7 Id.,403 ;
7 В. М. , 569 ; 8 Id., 42 ; 11 Bush , 507); or may refuseto give instructionsasked for and
give othersin theirstead. 5 B. M. , 42 ; 12 Id., 249.
And itisthe duty of the court, on motion of a party, to instructthejury, not only on
points of law presentedby the issues, but as to what would be the effectof a verdict, if
therebe a disputeon thequestion. Delanyv. Vaughn,3 Bibb,379.
2. Duty of courtas togivingwritteninstructions.
The failureof the court to reduce itsinstructionsto writing, when required to do so
by a party, isground forreversal. But,thecourthavinggivenoralinstructions,toldthe
jury thattheycouldretire,and thatthe instructionswould be reducedtowriting, and
thatiftheyhad any difficulty,theycouldsendforthem; and the plaintiff'scounselsaid
he hoped thatthe courtwould do so: held, thatthiswas not a requirementof written
instructions. 1 Met ., 83 .
3. Law tobe decidedby court- facts byjury.
The courtshoulddecideallquestionsof law,by instructions,and submit onlycontro-
vertedfactsto the jury. 2 Met ., 553.
Consequently,thecourthas therighttoinstructthejurythattheymustregardastrue,
forthepurposesof the action, a designatedfactwhich isnot controvertedby the plead-
ings(3 Met.,56); and itiserroneousto instructthejurythattheymust findthata note
suedon was executedby thedefendant,when theexecutionof itwas not put inissue(18
B. M. ,226); or thattheymay find, from the evidence,the non-existenceof a factthe
existenceof which isadmitted by the pleadings. 5 Bush ,474 .
And an instructionwhich submits a question of law to the jury is erroneous; as, an
instructionthat " the plaintiffmust eitherprove an express contractor one necessarily
impliedfromthecircumstances," withoutsayingfromwhat circumstancesa contractwould
necessarilybe implied(1 B. M. , 122); orthateverymaterialallegationof a pleading,not
controvertedby an adversepleading,must be taken astrue,withoutshowingwhat alle-
gationswere not controverted. I Met ., 570 ; 2 Id., 301 ; 7 Bush , 198.
4. Abstract instructions.
" Every propositionisdeemed abstract, however pertinentand applicableit may be,
unlessitbe made to apply in express terms eitherto the attitudeof the partiesor to the
veryfactsinissue." 7J.J. M. , 197.
And itiserroneous to give an instructionwhich , though correct as a matter of law, is
hypothecatedupon facts,or upon thenon-existenceof facts,which theevidencedoesnot
conduce to prove (I Mon., 126; 3 Id., 534; I Dana ,38; 11 B. M.,41; 13 Id.,486); or
which relatesto the weight of conflictingtestimony(I B. M. , 291 ; 17 Id., 28 ; 8 Bush ,

208; 11B. M. ,37); or to a matternot in issue(18B. M. ,744; I Duv ., 256); orwhich
relatesto an immaterialissueand iscalculatedto divert the attentionof the jury from a
materialissue(Reed v.Greathouse,7 Mon., 558); but, therebeing two immaterialissues,

1. TIME OF TAKING EXCEPTION .
Exception must be taken when the decision is rendered, notwithstanding a rule of
court to the contrary. Kennedy v . Cunningham , 2 Met ., 538.
But ,where the billof exceptions showed that defendant'sobjectionto a questionwas
overruled, and then statedthe testimony of the witness, which was followed by the state-
ment , " to allwhich the defendant excepted;" itwas held that itsufficientlyappeared that
the rulingwas excepted to when made . L. & N. R. Co. v .Ritter'sadm'r, 85 Ky .,368.
2. HOW BILL OF EXCEPTIONS TO BE FILED .
A billof exceptionscan not be filedin vacation, nor out of court (Freeman v . Bren-
ham , 17 B. M. , 603 ; Allard v .Smith, 2 Met ., 298 ; Corley'sex'r v .Evans and wife, 4
Bush ,409); though an order of court may have been made allowing it(Allardv .Smith,
2 Met ., 298); unlessthe order was made by consent of partiesentered of record. Kelsoe
v .Ellis, 10 B. М. , 38.
3. TIME OF FILING BILL OF EXCEPTIONS .
(1) The time for filinga billof exceptions,or forobtainingan extensionof time,is
reckoned from the overrulingof a motion for a new trial, and the court has no power ,
whilst such motion ispending , to prescribethe time forfilingsuch bill. Harper v .Harper ,
10 Bush ,447 .
(2) A billof exceptionswillnot be regardedby theCourtof Appealsunlessitbe filed
ortenderedon theday fixedby thelaw; or on orbeforethe day fixedby the court,ifthe
time be extended ; and the extension must be made by an order of record. Freeman v .

Id.,425 ; Meadows v . Campbell, I Bush , 104 ; Baileyv .Villier,6 Id., 27 ; Lynch v .Reynolds,
6 Id., 547; Seattv .Burrows , 13 Id., 450 ; Meaux v .Meaux , 81 Ky ., 475 ; L. & N. R.
Co. v . Turner , Id ., 489 .

and on thatday furthertime was given, without designating a day : held, that the latter
order was void, and that the billof exceptionsfiledwas no part of the record. Smith v.

(4) The rightof a partytoobjecttoa billof exceptions,becauseitisnot tenderedin
propertime,isnotwaivedby reasonof assistancegivenby hiscounselout ofcourtin the

(a) GENERAL RULES AS TO NEW TRIALS.
1. Motion and grounds tobe ofrecord.
The motion and grounds fora new trialcan only be filedby an order of court, entered
on the order-book ,and they should be indorsedfiled. 1 Mon. , 3 ; 78 Ky ., 536.
2. When a motionfor a new trialisnecessaryor allowable.
After a verdictby a jury,a motion for a new trialisnecessaryto enablea partyto
obtainfrom theCourt of Appealsa correctionof errorsastoadmittingor excludingevi-
dence, or givingor refusinginstructions(Humphrey v.Walton,2 Bush, 580; Detheragev.
Montgomery,4 Id.,46); and such motionisequallynecessarywhen ,inan ordinaryaction,
an issueof facthas beendecidedby the court,upon a submission,by waiveror otherwise,
18

of the law and facts(Helm v.Coffey,80 Ky ., 176,and Hendersonv.Dupree,82 Id.,680,
overrulingUnionIns.Co.ofLouisvillev.Groom,4 Bush,289); and insuchcasesthe appel-
lantshould requirethe court to " statein writing the conclusionsof factfound separately
from the conclusionsof law ." 332.
But such motion is not necessary in an action in equity (4 Bush , 292), or upon the
trialofan attachment(84 Ky .,27);and an appealmay be taken,withoutsuchmotion,even
aftera trialby jury, but itplacesonly mattersof recordbeforethe Court ofAppeals.
See note (a) 2 to 2 333.
But a motion to rehear a motion for a new trial, which has been overruled, is not
allowable; the only redressof the defeatedlitigantisby appeal. LouisvilleRock and Lime
Co. v .Kerr, 78 Ky ., 12.
3. Effectofa motionfor a new trial.
Such motion, if made before renditionof judgment , suspends proceedingson thever-
dict; as,theissuingof a writofinquiryastodamages (Wrightv.Haddock,7 Dana ,253);
and, ifmade afterjudgment, it suspendsproceedingsthereon; as, the issuingof an
executionor the prosecutionof an appeal(2 Dana ,335; LouisvilleChemicalWorks v.
Commonwealth , 8 Bush , 179); and the motion may be decided at a subsequent term ; but
judgment is notsuspendedby a motion to setasidean orderoverrulinga motionfora
new trial(78 Ky ., 14); and the court can not prescribethe time for filinga billof excep-
tionswhilstsuch motion ispending (10 Bush , 451-52); and itwas held that the death of
a defendant,afterjudgment and whilsthis motion fora new trialwas pending,did not
abatetheaction, though itwas upon a causeof actionwhich did not survive. Turner's
adm'r v .Booker, 2 Dana , 334.
A motion fora new trial, if overruled,does not prevent a new motion therefor, upon a
ground notembracedinthefirstmotion,ifa sufficientexcusebe made fornotsoembracing
it(Hughes v .McGee , 1 Mar. , 28); and a motion for a new trialdoes not prevent, nor isit
preventedby,a motion inarrestofjudgment. 7 Dana ,473.
4. Effectofgranting a new trial.
(1) A defendant,aftergettinga new trial,can not appealfrom the originaljudgment.
9 Bush , 69.
(2) Ifa judgment inan actionatlaw ordecreeinchancerybe improperlysetaside,on
a motionfora new trialora petitionfora rehearing,and a differentconclusionbe arrived
at in a secondjudgment or decree,the Court of Appealswillnot merelysetasidethe
latterbut willdirectthe former to be re-instated. 2 Bibb, 177 ; 6 Mon. , 243 ; 6 Bush ,664.
5. Weight given toopinion ofpresidingjudge.
" This courthasalwaysrequiredthatcauseshouldbe veryclearlymade out to justify
thereversalof a judgment forrefusaltogranta new trial." King v.Walker,1Mar.,314.
But inEwing v.Price,3J.J. M. ,520, inwhich theonlyground reliedon fora reversal
was thegrantingof a new trialto the appellee, the Court of Appeals,afterstatingfacts
which would havejustifiedthecourtbelow inrefusingthenew trial,said: " But thereis
a materialand obviousdistinctionin the grounds which willrequirethe reversalofa
judgmentoverruling,and onegranting,a new trial. By grantinga new trialthecaseisonly
kept open foranothertrialon themerits,and inthattrialeachpartyhasan equalchance
forjustice. But,by overrulinga motion for a new trial,the unsuccessfulpartyiscon-
cluded,and may be subjectedto irretrievablelossand irreparableinjustice. Therefore,
thiscourtwillbe more inclinedto sustaina judgment granting,than one overruling,a
new trial." And see Caldwellv.Wright,8 B. M. , 526; Ford v.Gregory'sheirs,10 Id.,
182; Guthriev .Bogart, I Mar. ,334.
6. Imposing termson applicationfor a new trial.
(1) Generally.
In an actionforassaultand battery(thecauseofactionforwhichdieswiththeperson),
thedefendantdiedwhilsthismotionfora new trialwas pending; and the courtbelow

Ithas been heldthat, in casesinwhich exemplarydamages are recoverable, a verdict

two months. Ewing thereafterinstitutedan actionof trespassagainstTyson for" taking
and carryingaway,keeping,and detaining" theslave,whose serviceswere proved to be

damages ; and , on a motion for a new trial, itwas held,I,that Ewing had a rightto main-
taintrespass,the law implyingforcefrom Tyson's employment of the slave; 2, thatthe
jury had a right to give " smart-money ;" and , 3, that the verdictwas " not so enormous
astoshow thatthejurymust have been influencedby passionor prejudice. Theirverdict
may have been the resultof deliberatejudgment on theirpart." But seeDanielv.Hol-
land, 4 J.J. M. ,27. And the courtrefusedto setasidea verdictfor$4,000forslander
(2 Met., 558),and for$5,000forseducingthe plaintiff'sdaughter(5 Bush, 567);and dili-
gentsearchhas not enabledme tofinda caseinwhich exemplarydamages were allowable

ielv.Holland,citedabove, istobe regardedassucha case. But inthatcase,asthecourt

(a) This subsectionrelatesnot to salesof land under judgments ,but to judgments for
land; as, in an action for specificperformance of a covenant to convey; and the rule

ousjudgments,doesnotapplytotitlesundererroneousjudgmentsforland,even theplain.

Id., 449 .
(b) 1. The Code of1854containedno provisionastoconfirmingsales,butdeclaredthat
" real property may be conveyed by a commissioner where such property has
been soldunder ajudgment or orderof thecourt,and the purchase-money paid" (2424).
But an order of confirmationwas necessaryunder the old practice(2 B. M. , 411); and , I,
itwas heldthatsuchan orderwas not equivalenttoan orderdirectinga conveyance(Gill
v .Hewitt, η Bush , 10); and , 2, the decision in Vance's adm'r v .Foster& Ray , 9 Bush ,
389, seems to authorizethe inferencethat, if a house and lot be sold under an order of
court, thepurchasermust pay the amount of his bid though the house be accidentally
destroyedby firebefore he receivespossessionand beforethe saleisconfirmed, therebeing
no objectionto confirmationexcept the destructionof the house.
2. The chancellor should not order a re-sale merely because there is an offerof an
advance of ten per cent. 9 Bush , 289. A decretal sale should not be set aside for
mere inadequacyof price; but " inthiscase,not onlywas the appellant'sbid flagrantly
inadequate,and,on thatground,ought not to have been confirmed,but the simplefact
thata confirmationwould have sacrificedthe interestof infantsentitledtothe guardian-
protectionof thecourtwas itselfample ground forquashingthesale." 1Bush, 13, 14.
" There must be eitherfraud or misconduct in some one connected with the sale; some
surpriseor misapprehension on the part of those interestedor of the officerwho conducts
thesale; or some irregularityintheproceeding,or othercircumstancesattendingit,con-
ducing to show unfairness, beforethe chancellorwillrefuseto confirm thisact of his com-
missioner." 9 Bush , 289.
" But when thepricebid isgreatlydisproportionedtotheactualvalueoftheproperty,
onlyslightadditionalcircumstancesare requiredtojustifyand make itthe duty of the
chancellorto set it aside. And where there is any fact connected with the sale from
which fraudmay be presumed,or when thedefendant,by casualtyor misfortune,hasbeen
preventedfrom appearingintheaction,ortakingstepstoavoida sacrificeofhisproperty,
itshould be setaside." 84 Ky .,693 .

In an actionbrought by A againsthis debtors,B ,thedefendant,was constructively
summoned ,and a tractof land belongingto him was attached. B mortgaged the land,
pendentelite, toC ; who , havingbeen made a defendant, filedan answer contestingthe
attachment,and prayingforan enforcementof the mortgage; and itwas held thatthe
attachmentwas voidforirregularities,and that,therefore,thecourthad no jurisdictionof
the actionor of the cross-petition. Petersv . Conway , 4 Bush , 571. In a note on page 39
ofnotestomy firsteditionof theCode Imade thesecomments on thatcase:
" But, query; because,though the attachmentwas void,itseems thatthecourthad
jurisdictionof theactionof A ; and might,on finalhearing,have ordereda saleof the
landforthepayment of A'sdemand ,aftersatisfyingthemortgage-debt. The courtseems
tohave believedthatan attachmentisnecessarytogivejurisdictionof an actionagainsta
defendant constructivelysummoned . This seems to be erroneous. An attachment isnot
an action; nor isa ground for an attachment a cause of action. Such ground need not be
stated in the petition; and , untilthe decisionwas rendered in Scottv.Doneghy , 17 Β .Μ .,
321,thereseemed tobe good reasonforbelievingthatsuchground couldbe statedonlyin
a separateaffidavit. This provisionalremedy ismerely ancillaryand incidentalto the
actionin which itissought. Itmay be had eitherat or afterthe commencement of the
action.' Moore v .Sheppard, 1 Met .,97. To make the jurisdictionof the court, as to an
action,depend upon the validityofan attachment obtainedin it,would reversethe rule,that
'the incidentfollowstheprincipal.' Section449 of the Code of 1854, of which 2418 ofthis
Code issubstantiallya copy,declared: 'No lienon thepropertyofa defendantconstructively
summoned shallbe createdotherwisethan by an attachment,as provided in chapter 3 of
title8, or by judgment .' It seems clear that the legislaturecontemplated that an action
againsta defendantconstructivelysummoned might proceedto judgment againsthim ;
and thatthejudgment might createa lienupon his property, though the plaintiffhad
failedto attachit. Itseemsdifficultto perceiveupon what principleitcan be held that
an abortiveefforttoobtainan attachment-liencan affectthejurisdictionof the court,or
the rightsof the plaintiff, as totheaction."
I now concede,however,thatthecourtwas rightinholdingthatithad no jurisdiction;
because it is clear that our courts can not assume jurisdictionof an actionformoney
againstan absentdefendantunlessit be shown thathe haspropertysubjectto thejuris-
dictionof the court (4 Dana , 146-47) ; and of course that factcan not be shown by a void
attachment; and I have learned, from examination of the record in the officeof the clerk
of the Court of Appeals , that I erred in assuming that the plaintiff'spetitionshowed that
the defendant had property subjectto the jurisdictionof the court; for it failedto allege
thathe had anypropertyinthisState,realor personal: hence,thoughthedecisionwas
right, the deduction of a want of jurisdictionfrom the invalidityof the attachment was a
dictum .

310 [TITLE X.PROCEEDINGS IN CERTAIN ACTIONS .
But itwas decidedin Grigsbyv .Barr , 14 Bush , 330, that our courtshave no jurisdiction
of an actiontosubjectthelandof a non-residentfordebt,althoughtheplaintiff'spetition
shows itto be subjectto the jurisdictionof the court, unlessitbe attached.
Itwas held,however,in Trabuev.Conners,84 Ky .,283,notonlythatsuchjurisdiction
existedwithoutan attachment,ifthe plaintiffhas obtaineda judgment againstthe non-
resident, with an execution returned " no property found ;" but that,if the land be de-
scribedin the plaintiff'spetition, hisactiongives him a lienon the property.
I submit the followingsuggestionsas to the two lastnamed cases:
1. As to Grigsbyv .Barr .*
The courtsaid: " If theproceedingmight be had,asinthisinstance, withoutbond or
attachment,the defendant might appear and open the judgment , and conclusivelyshow
thathe was not indebted to the plaintiffin any sum whatever ; that the pretended claim
was without foundation in fact,and although the plaintiffmight be insolvent,yet the title
of thepurchaserwould be good, and theplaintiffwithoutremedy. We can not believe
that the legislatureintended to offersuch facilitiesfor fraud and to sanctionsuch injus-
tice:" to which the answer is,that ?410, without making any exception,requiresthe plain-
tiffto give an indemnifying bond , " with good suretyapproved by the court," " before
judgment is renderedagainsta defendantconstructivelysummoned ,and who has not
appeared ."
The courtalsosaid,in substance,that,notwithstandingthewords " orby judgment"
in 2418 ; yet,as 2419 forbidsa personaljudgment againsta defendant constructivelysum-
moned ,and as ? 194 authorizesan attachment in an action against such a defendant, the
plaintiffcan not acquirea lienon his propertyexcept by an attachment. But , in an action
under title10,chapter4, upon a returnof " no propertyfound," the petitionhaving
described the property which the plaintiffsought to subject, itwas held that a mere
serviceof process on the defendant createda lienon the property, although 442 declares
that " a lien may be be created on the property of the defendant by the levy of an
attachment,or by serviceof thesummons ,with theobjectoftheactionindorsedthereon,
on thepersonholdingorcontrollinghisproperty."
The court,however, in supportof itsrulinginGrigsbyv.Barr,said: " All proceed-
ingstosubjectthepropertyofnon-residentsnotactuallyserved,and who do notappearto
theaction,areinderogationofthecommon law,and nothingistobe presumedinfavorof
thejurisdiction." Are not proceedingsupon returnsof " no propertyfound" in deroga-
tionof the common law? But,waivingthatquestion,I submitthat" allproceedingsto
subjectthe propertyof non-residentsnot actuallyserved,and who do not appeartothe
action," are not in derogationof the common law. Accordingto the common law,the
plaintiffin an actionat law,upon a writbeing returned" no inhabitant," could, by
theprocessofoutlawry,obtaina personaljudgment againstthedefendant, underwhich,
notonlypropertywhich was subjectto execution,but alsochosesin actionbelongingto
the defendant, could be subjected for the satisfactionof the demand . Bacon's Abridg.
ment, titleOutlawry. Our legislaturesubstitutedwarning ordersforoutlawry, and pro-
ceedingsinrem forproceedingsin personam (Scottv .McMillen , I Litt.,302 ; Moore v.Simpson,
5 Id.,49); without,however,authorizing,inallcases,theattachmentof personalproperty
(Moore v.Simpson, supra); and,priortothe act of February2, 1837(3 S. L., 12), there
was no statuteauthorizingthe attachmentof non-residents'landsfordebt. So faras I
can find, the statementof the court in Grigsbyv .Barr , as to what was the common law,
issupportedonlyby a dictumin Harris' heirsv.Bryants' exr's,7J. J. M. ,379- a dictum,
because,as thecourtdecided,thegeneralcourt,inwhich the actionwas brought, had no
jurisdictionof it in any aspectof the case. What was saidby the courtin Ferrillv.

And saidactwas our firststatuteon thissubject: yet,accordingto the decision(in 1822)

3. Proof .
No preliminaryproofof theplaintiff'sclaimon a note,or otherwrittencontractfor

a mortgage which recitesthat itismade to secure an indebtedness of $200,without speci-

But theplaintiff'sclaimon an accountmust be supportedby additionalproofshowing

sentative,and by no otherperson,and he refusetogivehisaffidavit. 1 Met.,601. And ,
itwas saidthat" everyfact,touchingthevalidityof theclaim,which, ina suitthereon,

It seems clearthat,subjecttomodificationscontainedin the Code (as,in 2474,which requiresplead-

changes made by subsequent statutes(as,an act of 1882[B. & F.'sGen. St.,565] which prescribesa form

ceedingson motions forsummary remedies,not only in the casesspecifiedin 444,but " in allother cases
speciallyauthorizedby statute." See Alexander ,& c.,v .Lewis,1 Met .,410.
And in Cooper v .Baker ,2 Bush ,244,the court,whilstholding that a purchaser of land soldunder a
decree can,by motion (which ,so faras I can find,no statuteauthorizes),compel a tenant to pay rent
which accrued afterthe purchase, said: " but in order to make that remedy available,the mode pre-
scribedby the severalsectionsof the CivilCode on the subjectshould be pursued." But the onlysections
of the Code citedby the court relateto the docketing of motions; from which itseems inferablethatthe
courtdid not regard the provisionsof thischapter as applicableto that motion; and the factsthat the
sections(347,352,353,355,356,and 362)which relateto motions,clearlyapply toallmotions,and that 444
only speaks of motions authorizedby itand by other statutes,seem to requirethe inferencethatthe pro-
visionsof Chapter V were intended to apply only to motionsthus authorized.

But itwas settledthat the act of 1798 gave to the court to which a statutoryaward

But, thoughparolevidenceisadmissibleto prove partialityor fraudon the part of

izetheinferenceofcorruptionorpartiality(Hardin,401; 2 Bibb,456-57; 4 Dana ,230-31);

An award which failstosettleeveryquestionsubmittedforsettlementisinvalid; as,an

II. What isa forcibleentry.- A forcibleentry is-
1. An entrywithoutthe consent of the person having the actual

writof forcibleentryisconcerned,forceisimpliedfrom an entrywithoutconsenton land

7J.J. M. ,604 ; 14 В. М. , 46); and ,a fortiori, thiswrit liesagainstone who enterswithout
havinga righttothepossession. 6 Bush,493.

Litt.S.C., 184.

3 Id., 397-98 .

A lessor'sheir or vendee can maintain forcibledetainer, ifthe lessorcould have done

And severaljointtenantsor co-parcenersmay join in a writ of forcibledetaineragainst

And , thoughtenantsin common can not joinin a writof forcibleentry,itisclear

§473 [522]. 1. Ifany personobtaina surveyoflandtowhichanother
claims a betterright,such othermay entera caveatwith the Register to
preventthe issuingof a grantuntilthe rightbe determined(a). The
caveatshallstatethe plaintiff'sclaimand the reasonswhy the grant
shouldnot issue(b). Itshallbe verifiedby his affidavit,or by thatof
hisagent(c),and declarethatitisenteredin good faith, with the in-
tentionof procuringthe landfortheplaintiff,and not forthe benefitof
the person againstwhom itisentered.

sixtydays from thetime itisentered, be lodgedwith the clerkof the
circuitcourtof the county where the landor the greaterpartthereof
lies. Failureso to lodge such copy shallbe deemed an abandonment of
the caveat; and itmay be disregardedby the Register,upon the clerk's
certificateof such failurebeing filedin his office.
3. If such copy be lodgedwithinsaidperiod,itshallbe treatedas a
petition,and the proceedingsupon itshallbe the same, includingan
appealto theCourt of Appeals,as thoseinan ordinaryaction(d ).
4. If the summons be not returned in due time, or be returned not
executed ,the caveat shallbe dismissed,if it be shown that the non-
executionor non-returnwas procuredby the plaintiffor resultedfrom

delivered into the Land Office within six months from the time it was

against the grant .
6. No grant shallissue, to the land in contest,to the plaintiffin
the caveat, or to another forhis use, untilthe caveat be dismissedor de-
cided(e); and any such grant,to the extentof such land,shallbe void.
7. If theplaintiffdo not prosecutehiscaveatas hereinrequired, or
ifthe same be dismissedor decided againsthim,neitherhe nor any
other forhis use shallhave another caveat againstthe same grant.

ceeding .

In general,mandamus does not liein favor of an applicantwho has some other appro-

177. Nor does it lie against even an executive or ministerialofficerforthe purpose of

tomake an allowancetothecountyjudge,eventhoughitbe a judicialdiscretionforabuse
ofwhich theremay be some otherremedy. 18 B. M. , 15,16; 79 Ky .,267.

(a) SURVIVORSHIP OF RIGHTS OF ACTION .
I. To plaintiffs.
1. As tojointtenantsor owners.
Inan actionatlaw by A and theexecutorofB ,on C'spromisetopay money toA and
B,the courtsaid: " By the common law,jointrightssurviveto,and become vestedin,
thesurvivingjointtenant. But,fortheencouragementof tradeand husbandry,itisheld
thatstockon a farm,thoughoccupiedjointly, and alsostockused ina jointundertaking
byway of partnershipintrade,shallalwaysbe consideredascommon ,and not asjoint
property; and thereshallbe no survivorshiptherein. This,however , must be
understoodof the rightofpropertyonly; for, althoughthe duty does not survive, the
remedydoes,and thereforethe survivormay sue alone; and when he recovers,he shall
accountwiththe executorof the deceasedpartnerforhisshare. The executorand sur-
vivorcannotjoininan actionagainstthe debtor;" and itwas heldthatthe defendant's
promisetoA and B jointly" must be consideredas a partnershiptransaction;" that,
though,on thedeathofB ,therightofpropertydid notwhollysurvivetoA , therightof
actiondid; and that a judgment in favorof the plaintiffswas erroneous(Morrisonv.
Winn ,Hardin , 480 ; acc.3 Mon. , 281-82); and that rulewas held to apply to actionsin
equityfor debts due to a firm. Litt. S. C., 495 ; 9 В. М. , 187. But , upon the dis-
solutionof a partnership by death, the personal representativeof the deceased partner
becomes tenantin common with the survivorof allthe partnershipproperty and effectsin
possession, therebeing a distinctionbetween such property in possessionand mere choses
inaction,debts,and otherrightsofactionbelongingtothepartnership[rightstosuefor],
which vestin the survivor." 13 B. M. , 413 .
"
In general,however , according to the common law , upon the death of a jointowner
ofproperty,whether the titlewas legalor equitable, not onlythe rightto sue forthe
property,but the right to the property, vested in the survivor, without liabilityto the
representativesof the decedent(Litt.S.C.,494-95; 6 Mon., 17); and the rightof joint
obligeesina bond toconveylandformedno exceptiontothatrule. Barclayv.Hendricks'
heirs,3 Dana ,378. And under the act of 1796 (M. & R., 876) which abolishedthejus
accrescendi,itwas heldthat,though upon thedeathofa jointtenanttherightof property
vestedinhis representativesas tenants in common with the survivor, the rightto sue (at
law) vestssolelyin the survivor; because, said the court, " itwould make strange con.
fusionthatone plaintiffshouldsueinhisown rightand anotherin another's" (Morrison
v.Winn ,Hardin, 482; Adie v.Cornwall,3 Mon.,276); and the same rulewas held to
applytoan actioninequity. Litt. S.C.,495 : contra, 2 Mar., 193.
There areseveraldistinctionsbetweenthe actof 1796 and theGeneralStatutes,as to
jointtenants: 1,theformerdestroyedsurvivorshipeven astothe estatesof executorsor
trustees(7 Mon. , 54).whilst the latterdoes not (G. S., ch. 63, art. 1, § 14); 2, the former
did not applyto an estateconveyedtoa husband and wife(I Dana ,37, 243),whilstthe
latterdoes(G. S.,ch.52,art.4, 13); 3, the latterdeclares,as theformerdid not, that
thedeathof a jointtenantshallnot " affectthe mode of proceedingupon anyjointcon-
tractorjudgment" (G. S.,ch.63,art.1, §14); and,4, the formerdeclared,as the latter
does not, that, upon the death ofjointtenants,theirestatesshallpass by descentor
devise " in the same manner as if such deceased joint tenants had been tenantsin com

370 [TITLE XI .REVIVOR OF ACTIONS .
mon ;" but that omission is immaterial, because the passing of a joint tenant'sinterest
to his representativesor deviseesnecessarilymakes them tenants in common with the
survivor .
The factthat, under the Code , as formerly,jointtenants must join in an action, for
jointproperty,does not prove that,if one should die pendentelite,the other can not pro-
ceed alone,being responsibletothedecedent'srepresentativesforwhat he may recover.
The onlydifficultyabout followingthe rulewhich prevailedunder the actof 1796,asto

II . THE BURTHEN OF PROOF IN ACTIONS FOR MALICIOUS PROSECUTIONS .
1. As toactionsfor maliciousprosecutionsofcriminalproceedings.
According to the opinion in Ullman v .Abrams ,9 Bush , 738, the nature of the defence
in which does not appear, the burthen lieson the plaintiffto prove both that the action
was maliciousand that itwas without probable cause.
It is clearthat the plaintiffmust allegeboth malice and want of probable cause (see
note(2)ante,page 89),and theburthenof proofison him astoboth allegations,ifthey
are traversedby the defendant; and such was the ruleupon the plea of not guilty, under
the old practice. 4 J.J. M. ,629; 5 B. M. , 546. But thatpleaisnot allowableunder
the Code (see note (2) a, ante, page 121),and the defendant must traverseeach allegation;
or he may , even without traversingthe allegationof malice, filea specialplea of probable
cause (see4 J. J. M. ,628-29); for,asallthedecisionson thesubjecthold,theplaintiff
can not recover unless both malice and want of probable cause be shown ; and , conse-
quently, the questionof malice isimmaterial, if probable cause be shown .
However ,notonlymay thejuryinfermalice(5 B. M. , 546),but thereisa primafacie
legalpresumptionof malice,from thewant of probablecause(Litt.S. C., 106; 7 Bush,
390); but the law does not implymalicefrom thefactthattheplaintiffwas acquittedby
theverdictof a jury,thoughitisconclusiveevidenceof hisinnocence. 4 J.J. M. ,630.
On the other hand , the law implies probable cause from the factthat the plaintiffwas
indictedby a grand jury,unlessupon thetestimonyof the prosecutoralone(Garrardv.
Willett,4J.J. M. ,628); and a judgment ina civilcase,againsta defendantwho appeared
and was heard,has been held to be conclusiveevidenceof probablecause, though the
judgment may have been reversedby theCourtofAppeals(12 B. M. ,555; 18 Id.,846†);
and I perceiveno reasontodoubt thatthe same ruleappliestoa judgment of conviction
in a criminal case .
But want of probable cause can not be inferredfrom proof of malice in a prosecution
for crime (I B. M. , 359); for, " in this respect, even malice becomes one of the safe-
guardsof society,and istheoccasionof bringingoffenderstojusticewho would otherwise
escape." Maddox v .McGinnis , 7 Mon. , 372.
As a plea of probable cause for a criminal prosecution, if sustained, constitutesa
defence,though the prosecutionmay have been malicious; and assucha pleaimpliedly
admitsmalice, and, ifunsustained, entitlesthe plaintiffto at leastnominaldamages,
the burthen of proofison the defendant(4 J. J. M. ,631; 3 Bush,83); though the
plaintiffhas the rightto prove factsshowing actualmalice(9 Bush ,741), in orderto
enhance the damages in the event of the defendant'sfailureto sustainhisdefence. Gar-
rard v.Willett, 4J. J. M. , 628 ; Caskey v . Lewis, 15 B. М. , 31 ; L. & N. R. Co.v .Brown ,
13 Bush ,475 .
For the requisites of such plea , see 7 Mon. , 401 ; and as to the requisite evidence to support such
plea , see 3 B. М. ,6.
† But see Francis v .Burnett , cited in note 6,ante ,page 225 .

2. As toactionsfor maliciousprosecutionsof civilproceedings.
In Maddox v.McGinnis , supra, publicpolicy, namely , the bringing of criminalsto jus-
tice,isstatedas the reasonforholdingthatwant of probablecausefora criminalprose-
cutioncan not be inferredfrom proof of the prosecutor'smaliceagainstthe accused. But
surelypublicpolicydemands thediscouragementof maliciousprosecutionsof civilpro-
ceedings; and,therebeing no decisionon thesubjectsofarasI can find,itseems tome
thattheinferenceof want ofprobablecause from proof oftheprosecutor'smaliceought
to be allowed .
And , indeed, itseems to me that, in order to discourage maliciousprosecutionsof civil
proceedings,maliceand want ofgoodcauseforsucha prosecutionought togivea causeof
action; but ithas been repeatedlyheld thatboth maliceand want of probablecause
must be allegedby theplaintiffinsuchan action(2 Dana ,425; 5 B. M. ,546; 8 Id., 52,
53; 10 Id.,20; 1 Met.,240; 13 Bush,633); and,assuming suchtobe the law,itseems
thatthe rulesasto the burthenof proofin actionsformaliciouscriminalprosecutions
apply to actionsfor malicious prosecutionsof civilproceedings, unless the inferenceof
want of probable cause from proof of malice be allowed as an exception.
III . THE BURTHEN OF PROOF IN WILL -CASES .
The controversieson this subject have related, I, to the testator'ssanity; and , 2, to

Though thecommon law presumptionisthateverypersonhasa sound mind ; yet,as
the power to devisepropertyispurelystatutory(4 Mon. ,201),and as the statutegives
that power only to a " person of sound mind , not being under twenty-one years of age,
nor a married woman ," except that a married woman may by will dispose of her separate

statutoryqualifications, including a " sound mind " (13 В. М. , 269-70); but the opinions
of witnesses,thatthe testatorwas of sound mind,are not indispensable,forthe making
of a rationalwill,especiallyifwrittenby the testator,is sufficientprima facieproofof
sanity. 4 Bibb,244; 13 B. М. , 270; 13 Bush, 170; 83 Ky .,261.
And theprobateof a willin thecountycourtdoes not relievethe propoundersfrom

But the presumptionof sanityhas not been abolishedexcepttothe extentof casting

§ 592 [656]. Orderinwhich proofismade on trial.-The partywho
begins the case must , ordinarily, exhaust his evidence before the other
begins. But the orderof proof shallbe regulatedby the court, so as
to expedite the trial,and enable the tribunalto obtain a clearview of
the whole evidence .
As totheorderof proofsee,further, 317,subs.3.
§ 593 [657]. Controlof courtovermode of interrogation.-The court
shallexercisea reasonablecontroloverthe mode of interrogation, so
as to make itrapid, distinct, as littleannoying to the witness, and as
effectiveforthe extractionof the truth, as may be ; but, subjectto this
control,thepartiesmay put such legaland pertinentquestionsas they
may seefit. The court, however, may stopthe productionof further
evidenceon a particularpoint, ifthe evidence upon itbe alreadyso
fullas to preclude reasonabledoubt .
§ 594 [658]. Directand cross-examination.-The examinationof a
witnessby the partyproducing him is the directexamination; his
examination,upon the same matter, by the adversepartyisthecross-
examination. The direct examination must be completed before the
cross-examination begins, unlessthe court otherwise direct.
§ 595 [659]. Leading questions.-A questionthat suggeststo the
witnesstheanswerwhich the examiningpartydesiresisa leadingques-
tion. On the directexamination,leadingquestionsare not allowed,
exceptunder specialcircumstancesmaking itappearthatthe interests
of justicerequireit. On cross-examination,theadversepartymay put
leadingquestions; but, ifhe examine the witnesson new matters, such
examinationissubjectto the same rulesas the directexamination.
As to leadingquestions, see note to ? 579.

Upon an appeal,on the ground thatthedefendanthad not been summoned ,itwas

But, inJones, & c., v .Edwards , & c., 78 Ky .,6 , (decided in 1879), itwas held that a

ago," could not be collaterallyattacked on the ground that the record failedto show that

" Under our system, itnever has been requiredthat evidence of the serviceof the process
shouldbe preservedin any permanent form. Processisreturnedand placedamong the

voidunlessthesummons can be produced,no matterhow longtheperiodduringwhich it
hasstoodunquestioned,the consequences would be of the most seriouscharacter.
Itought,atany rateaftera greatlapseof time, tobe presumed thattheprocesshasbeen
lost,ratherthan that the court rendered judgment without process." But the provisions

§ 679 [750]. Majoritymay act. An authority conferred by law

ber of days be requiredto intervenebetween two acts, the day ofone
onlyof the actsmay be counted.
" The ruleiswell settledthat where the computation isto be made from the actdone ,
then the day on which itisdone must be included, but, if to be made from the day itself,
theday must be excluded." 80 Ky .,306,and casescited; 11Bush, 220. For a lengthy

theclerkisnot a defectivebond forwhich a new one can be substituted. Id., 131.

RULES AS TO APPEALS FROM JUSTICES' COURTS , &C .
1.As towhat arefinaljudgments, from which only appeals lie, see note I to 2734 .
2. As to the “ amount in controversy."
As thecasemust be " triedanew as ifno judgment had been rendered," the amount in
controversyin the court appealed to, whether the appeal be taken by the plaintiffor the
defendant,isnot necessarilytheamount ofthejudgmentappealedfrom,but istheamount
whichwas incontroversyinthecourtappealedfrom. Millsv.Cauchman,4J.J. M. ,242;
Williamsv.Wilson,5 Dana ,596. Thus,inan actionina justice'scourtfor$90 damages,
theplaintiffrecoveredjudgment for$50,and on the defendant'sappealitwas heldthat
$90wastheamount incontroversy. Donahue v.Murray, 2 Bush, 194.
And , ifthe plaintiffsue for$ 50 or lessand the defendant plead a set-offfor more than
$50,theset-offistheamount incontroversy. Parkerv.Hulme,3 Dana ,499.
3. As toassumptionofjurisdictionby thecourtappealedto.
Accordingto Elligev. Wilson,1 J. J. M. , 588,ifan appealbe taken to the circuit
court,which should have been taken to the quarterlycourt, itwould be the duty of the
circuitcourttodismisstheappealand remittheappelleeto hisjudgment beforethejus-
tice; butitwas held inHughes v.Hardesty, 13 Bush,364,thatthe circuitcourtmay try
thecaseunlessitsjurisdictionbe objectedto. The circuitcourtcan not,however,be
compelled,by agreementof parties,to trya casewhich ispending,upon appeal,in the
quarterlycourt; the proceedingwas properlydismissed. Davisv.Davis, 10 Bush,274.
4. Who may takean appeal, and itseffect.
(1) Eitherof severaldefendantsagainstwhom a judgment has been renderedmay
appeal,and theappealbringsbeforethecourtappealedtoallthosedefendants(Palmerv.

458 [TITE XVI .INFERIOR COURTS .
Kennedy ,& c., 7 J. J. M. ,498); but not persons who were not made defendants in the
courtappealedfrom,eitherby summons or appearance. Claggetv.Blanchard,8 Dana ,42.
(2) A partyagainstwhom judgment isrenderedina justice'scourt,withoutsummons ,
may appealand reverseitwithoutsubjectinghimselftoa judgment inthecourtappealed
to . Claggetv .Blanchard, 8 Dana , 43 .
(3) When a cause isremoved by appeal from a justice'scourt to the circuitcourt, the
justicehas no further power over it (Palmer v . Kennedy , 7 J. J. M. , 500 ; Shadrack v .
Milam ,6 Id.,608); unlessthe appeal be dismissed: seenextfollowingnote.
5. Dismissal ofappealor actionby thecourt.
The dismissalof an appeal reinstatesthe partieswhere they stood when itwas taken,
and deprivesthe appellatecourtofany power overthe case. Milesv.Reed,2J.J. M. ,
137; Manierv. Lindsay,3 Bush,95 ; Olmsteadv.Mason,Id.,693.
And such dismissalisproperiftheappellanthas failedto give an appeal-bond as is
requiredby law (Bledsoev. Cassady,2 Mar.,459; Manier v. Lindsay,3 Bush,95); or if
the appellant'swarrantwas properlydismissedby the courtappealedfrom, forwant of
jurisdiction(Fidlerv. Hall,2 Met.,461); orifthe courtappealedto has no jurisdiction
of the appeal. Elligev. Wilson, 1 J. J. M. , 588; Milesv.Reed, 2 Id., 137; Jonesv.
Thompson's ex'r, 12 Bush , 394.
But,ifthecourtappealedfrom had not jurisdictionof thesubjectof theaction,and
the appellatecourthas jurisdictionof the appeal,itshouldnot dismissthe appealand
thusplacethepartieswhere theystoodbeforeitwas taken; but shoulddismisstheaction
forwant ofjurisdictionin the courtappealedfrom,therebyvacatingitsjudgment and
remittingtheplaintifftohisproperremedy by a new action. McKitrickv.Peter,5 Dana ,
587; Bassetv. Oldham,7 Id., 168; Howard v.Jones,2 B. M. , 526; Wiggintonv.Moss,2
Met .,38 ; Fleming v . Limebaugh ,Id.,265 ; impliedlyoverrulingPollardv .Holeman ,4 Bibb,
416 , in which it was held to be the duty of the circuitcourt to try the case, de novo,
thoughthecourtappealedfrom had nojurisdiction.
6. Dismissal of appeal by appellant.
A defendant who takes an appeal has no right to dismissitwithout the consent of the
plaintiff, who isentitledto have a trialon the merits(Semplev.Morrison, 7 Mon., 298);
and itseemsnecessarilytofollowthata plaintiffwho takesan appealhas no righttodis-
miss itwithout the defendant'sconsent. And see 730 of Code .
7. Non -suitoftheplaintiff.
In Shadrackv.Milam,6 J. J. M. ,608, itwas held that,upon an appealby the de-
fendant,the appellee,who stoodin the appellatecourtas plaintiffin the cause,had a
right," beforethejuryhad retiredfrom the bar toconsiderof theirverdict" (seeCode,
371),totakea non-suit; but thatno executioncouldbe issuedon thejudgment appealed
from .
8. Irregularitiesin proceedingsof, or before, thecourtappealedfrom .
An act of 1812 (M. & B.,894),declaredthat" no appealshallbe dismissedforany
irregularityintheproceedingshad beforethemagistrate; but thesame shallbe triedupon
itsmeritsasthough no trialhad been previouslyhad thereon."
And itwas held that, upon an appeal to the circuitcourt, it was immaterial that the
warrant issuedby thejusticedid not name a day for the defendant'sappearance, or
speciallydesignatethe causeofaction(Bledsoev.Cassady,2 Mar.,461); or thatthejudg-
ment was renderedby a justiceinwhose districtthedefendantdid not reside(Sturgus'
adm'rv . White'sadm'r,7 Mon.,228); or" whether thejudgment of thejusticewas void
or only voidable, or whether therewas or was (not a] warrant." Burtonv. Longs, 3
Litt., 444 .
Itseemsprobablethattheprovisionof the actof 1812concerningirregularitiesisstill
in force, as no provisionon that subjectseems to be contained in eitherthe Code or the
General Statutes. It is clear, at any rate, that objection to sufficiencyof notice of a

Upon an appeal from a justice'scourt, unlessfor want of a summons of the defendant

merits(Bennettv. Thompson, 10Bush,365); and may ,even though the appealhas been
taken by the defendant,renderjudgment in favorof the plaintiffformore than the amount
ofthejudgment appealedfrom. Millsv. Cauchman,4 J.J. M. , 242; Williamsv. Wilson,
5 Dana ,596; Donahuev. Murray,2 Bush, 194.
Nor doesthejudgment ofthejusticeaffecttheburden ofproof,astowhich theparties
standas they would have stood had the action been commenced in the circuitcourt (Suter

And thecauseofactiontriedin thecircuitcourtmust be thesame (Burbagev.Squires,

Nor cantheplaintiffsoincreasehisdemand astoexceedthejurisdictionofthejustice's
court. 3 Met ., 79.
But,though the plaintiffcan not presenta new causeof actionin the circuitcourt,

Section734 declares that an appeal shallbe granted " to a party or privy against a
partyor privy."
1. As toparties.
The following have been held tobe partieswho can appeal: One through whose land a

landsued for,pendentelite(7 J. J. M. , 302,642); one whose land was sold whilst he was

forinfants,though theirstatutoryguardianmay have acceptedand receiptedfor the
money towhich theywere entitledunderthejudgment appealedfrom (8 Bush,69); a gar-
nishee, thoughnotmade a defendant,againstwhom an attachingcreditorhas obtaineda
judgment (17 B. M. ,631; Smithv.Gower, 3 Met., 171); a creditorwho has proved a
claim in an action brought under the act of 1856 (Heidrich & Co. v . Silva, & c., 11
Ky .L. R. ,645); a bidder for property at a decretal sale whose bid is rejectedby the
court(1 Bush , 12 ; 9 Id., 285 ; Hughes v .Swope, 11 Ky . L. R. , 783), or who is compelled
tocomplywith his bid, notwithstandinghis exceptions(3 Bush, 491; Pittman,& c.,v.
Wakefield,& c., 11 Ky . L. R., 972) ; and a city, though the actionwas against itsmayor
and council. 18 В. М. , 13.
And an appeal from an order appointing a committee for a person of unsound mind
must be prosecuted in his name . 5 Dana , 59.
And though, in general, a partywhose proceedingshave been so erroneousthata
judgment in his favorwould have been reversedcan not obtaina reversalof a judg-

III . NECESSARY PARTIES TO APPEALS .
1. As toappellants.
In Wellsv.Wells, 4 Mon. , 152, itwas held that each party in a will case had a rightto
maintain a separatewrit of error, and that, " although more than one may join,yet the
writ is to be treated as a several writ ."
But , ingeneral, allpartiesagainstwhom a jointjudgment isrendered must join in an
appeal to reverse it (2 Bibb, 392 ; 1 Mar. , 22 ; 1 Dana , 366 ; I R. M. , 69); unless some
refusetojoin,inwhich event,underthe old practice,the partydesiringa reversalprose-
cuted a writ of error, and " then, by summons and severance,he might proceed on his own
casealone,and exempt himself from any eventual liabilityfor the others." 2 Bibb, 392 ;
2 J. J. M. , 72. Why the proceeding of summons and severancewas not allowed upon
appeals, I have not seen stated, but itseems clear thatitwas not allowed because appeals
were granted only by the court renderingthe judgment . It seems clear, at any rate, that

(e) As towhat constitutesa judgment .
Upon thissubjecttherehas been some conflictofopinion. In Brucev.Fox, I Dana ,
449, the courtsaid: " Discretionaryacts(such, for example, as an appointmentof a
clerkorthe removal ofa guardian)which do not establishor violateany right,defined
and guarranteedby law,arenotjudicial,and are,therefore,notdirectlyrevisableby this
court ."
Thus ,in Piattv . Alloway, 2 Bibb, 554, itwas held that a writ of error did not lieto
an orderofthe countycourtremovinga guardian,and the courtsaid: " The removalof
an officer,the tenure of whose officeis the will of a superior, isin allgovernments con

476 [TITLE XVIII.APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS .
sideredan executive function. A guardian being a privateand not a public officerdoes
not change the characterof the power exerted in hisremoval. Whether the officebe public
or private, the nature and characterof the power of removal will be the same ." And in
Taylor v. Commonwealth , 3 J. J. M. , 401, itwas held that a writ of errorwould not lieto
an order of the county court declaring that A had vacated the officeof clerkof saidcourt
and appointingB to fillthe supposedvacancy,astheactionof the courtwas executive
and not judicial. But in Bruce v. Fox,citedsupra, a majorityof the courtheld that
ordersof the circuitcourtrefusingto qualifyB and qualifyingF ,as Commonwealth's
attorney,were judicialorders. And in Isaacsv. Taylor, 3 Dana ,600, ordersof the county
court removing A and appointingB , as guardian, were held to be judicialorders. And
thatdecisionwas citedwith approvalinGorham v. Luckett,6 B. M. ,146,inwhich itwas
held that, under a statuteauthorizing county courtsto remove jailers" whenever itshall
appeartothem thatsuch jailershave been guiltyofneglectof duty," an orderremoving
a jailerwas a judicialorder. And , under a statuteauthorizingeach countycourttosus-
pend thejailerof the county " from actingas such, for such period as itmay deem right,
when the public interestshallimperativelyrequirethe same ," an order suspending a jailer
was held to be a judicialorder. Lowe v . Commonwealth , 3 Met ., 237; and see Smith v .
Cochran,& c.,7 Bush , 147. And itissettledthat an appointment or removal of an admin-
istratorisa judicialorder. 6 B. M. , 146, and cases cited. And an order disbarringan
attorney, even by a court acting merely ex officio, isa judicialorder. I Dana ,450 , argu-
endo; Rice v . Commonwealth , 18 B. M. ,472 .
In Taylor v . Commonwealth , cited supra, the court said: "Although the Constitution
has confided to the courts the appointment of their own clerks, stillthe nature of the
power is not changed . It is essentiallyexecutive, whensoever or by whomsoever it may
be exercised. It is as much executivewhen exercisedby a court as by the governor ."
But inGorham v.Luckett,citedsupra,thecourtsaid: " The law definesand regulatesthe
rightsand interestsof individuals,onlyby declaringor determiningthe factson which
those rightsand interestsare to depend , and by which they are to be decided. Any
legitimateact which directlyaffectssuch rightsand interests, and establishesor destroys
them , necessarilyimpliesa decision upon the factsand the law upon which those rights
are made to depend ; and , therefore, every such act is in itselfessentiallyjudicial, by
whatever tribunalit may be exercised. If the power of doing such an act be vested in
the executive department of the government , the act itselfisnot the lessjudicialin its
nature or essence, but the power under which it is done being, in the distributionof
power , vested in the executive and not in the judiciary, itis, in the constitutionalsense,
an executiveand nota judicialpower,and,beingentrustedsolelytothejudgment of the
executive, iswithdrawn from the controlof the courts. But the same power, when
vested in a court, is not only in essence but in form ,and in the constitutionalsense, a
judicialpower . If vested in a tribunal of a mixed character, like the county
court, it is to be referredto itscharacteras a judicialbody , because it is in itsnature
and effecta judicialpower. And , if the questionwere doubtful, the power itselfor
an act done under it ought to be regarded as judicial, rather than to close against the
party aggrieved by itsabuse the common remedy by appeal or writ of error." 6 В. М. ,
160, 164.
It has been saidthat no appealliesfrom a decisionupon a writof habeascorpus,
because " thejurisdictionof thiscourt only extends to the finalorders and judgments of
inferiorcourts,and not to theordersorjudgmentswhich judicialofficersareauthorizedto
make out of court." 15 B. M. , 153. But under a statutewhich authorized the condem-
nation of land fora turnpike company by proceedings before a judge out of court, itwas
held that his actswere judicialand subject to revisionby the Court ofAppeals . Rice,
& c.,v .Danville, Lancaster& NicholasvilleTurnpike Company , 7 Dana , 81.

factsthat, accordingto the common law,a writingunder sealraiseda conclusivepre-
sumption of a sufficientconsideration, and that our statutesraisedunsealed writings to
the dignityof sealedwritingsand allowedthe considerationof eithertobe impeached.
See actsof 1801 and 1812, M. & B. , 331, 343; and G. S., ch.22, 22 , 3 .
The onlycasesthatoccurtome inwhich itisnecessaryforthe petitiontomake any

II. Petitionson contractscontainingconditionssubsequent(frequently
calledcollateralconditions).
a. Petition(in what would have been an actionof debt at common law) on a bond for pay-
ment ofmoney, conditionedtobevoidon performanceof an actby theobligor(a).
(Caption as in Form 1.)
The plaintiff,A. B., saysthatthe defendant,C. D., isindebtedtohim inthe sum of
one thousand dollars, for that the defendant by a writing [statedate,& c., as in Form 1],
promised topay theplaintiffone thousanddollarson the Istday of June, 1890,but that
he has notpaidany partthereofnor any interestthereon.
Wherefore the plaintiffasksforjudgment againstthe defendantforsaidmoney and
interestthereonfrom June 1st, 1890, and costs[and any otherreliefthe plaintiffmay
appear entitledto].
(No verificationnecessary.) E. F., Attorney.
(a) At common law,theobligeeina bond stipulatingforthe payment of a sum of
money ,but conditionedto be void on the performanceof an act by the obligor,could
maintain eitheran actionof debt or an actionof covenant ; for, in legal contemplation,
the conditionconstitutesa covenant of the obligorto perform the act specifiedin the con-
dition. Indebt,the plaintiffsuedforthe stipulatedsum withoutnoticingthe condition,
theburthen of pleadingwhich was on the defendant; and, ifhe failedto show per-
formance,judgment was renderedagainsthim forthestipulatedsum ; and,ifitconstituted
a penaltyin contemplationof a courtof equity,the defendantwas compelledtogo into
chanceryforrelieffrom thejudgment atlaw; but undertheCode (??17 and 113,subs.2),
the defendant, in order to availhimself of that equitableground of relief, must relyon
itin the actionat law . In covenant, however , the plaintiffwas bound to set forth both
thepromisetopay thestipulatedsum and thecondition,and toaver, notnon-payment of
thestipulatedsum, but,non-performanceof the condition; and ifithad notbeen per-
formed,he was entitledto a judgment fordamages .
Under the Code ,when the stipulatedsum constitutes, not a penalty, but, liquidated
damages ,it is,perhaps, immaterialwhether the plaintiffsetsforth the condition or not,
as, in eithercase, ifthe conditionhas not been performed,he is equitablyas well as
legallyentitledto a judgment forthe stipulatedsum ; but when that sum constitutesa
penalty,as the plaintiffisequitablyentitledtorecoverdamages only, hisrightto dam-
agesconstituteshiscauseof action, and he must setforththe promisetopay thestipu-
latedsum and the conditionand the defendant'sfailureto perform it,and pray forthe
damagesresultingtherefrom. See noteII,ante,page 70,and thenextfollowingform.
As towhat constitutesa penalty and what constitutesliquidateddamages , see Hen-
dersonv .Stainton, Hardin , 118 ; Rumsey ,& c., v .Matthews, 1 Bibb, 242 ; Taul v . Everet, 4
J. J. M. , 10 ; Applegatev .Jacoby, 9 Dana , 208 ; Howard v . Chiles, 8 B. M. , 378 ; E. &
P. R. Co. v .Geoghegan, 9 Bush , 56 ; Bell v . Truit, Id., 257 ; Thomasson v . Townsend , 10
Id.,116 ; Hahn v .Horstman , 12 Id., 249.

518 FORMS .
(a) This form ,includingthe bracketed partsof it(the brackets having been inserted
by me ), is a copy of a petitionconcerning which the court said, in Germania Insurance
Co. v .Rudwig , & c.,80 Ky ., 223 : " The petitioncontainsthe usual averments which are
essentialtoa cause of actionon such a policy."
Rudwig's actionwas on a policywhich contained provisionsthat itshould " cease and
be void and of no effect," ifthe representationsof the assured, on the faithof which the
contractwas founded,shouldbe foundinany respectuntrue; or ifhe should,withoutthe
consentof thecompany,pass beyond thelimitsoftheUnitedStates,& c.;or ifhe should
engageintheserviceof any railroad,& c.; orshoulddieby hisown hand orby thehands
of justice, & c.: thatcase isauthority, therefore, that such provisionsconstituteconditions
subsequent, which may furnishmatters for defence but need not be noticed in the plain-
tiff'spetition.
But , as will be shown (because I am unwilling to publish a defectiveor misleading
Form without explanation), Rudwig's petitionmade , not only several unnecessary state-
ments ,but one erroneous omissionto which , however , the attentionof the court does not
seem to have been called.
(6) These bracketedavermentsare clearlyunnecessary, the power to contract, & c.,
being necessarilyembraced by the power to do a general life-insurancebusiness.
(c) Thisavermenthasbeen heldtobe unnecessary,notonlyinan actionby theinsured,
but inan actionby the insureron thedefendant'scontracttopay premiums (seeMay on
Insurance, 590); and I submit that, as has been held in severalcases (see May on Insur-
ance, 577), thedefendantwould be estoppedfrom takingadvantageof itsown wrong
by showing thatthe contractwas made in violationof our statutorylaws relatingto
foreigninsurancecompanies: such rulingwould conform, and rulingto the contrary
would not conform , to that public policywhich forbidsjudicialenforcement of such con-
tractsin actionsbroughtby foreigninsurancecompanies,as to which see FranklinIns.
Co.v .Louisvilleand Ark . Packct Co., 9 Bush , 590. It seems clear, at any rate, that the
burden of allegingand proving that the insurerhas done itsduty by complying with those
laws ought not to be thrown on the insured.
(d ) A person can not lawfullyinsureanother'slifefor his own benefit, unlesshe have
" a reasonableground, founded on the relationsof the parties,eitherpecuniaryor of
blood or affinity, to expect some benefitor advantage from the continuance of the lifeof
the assured" (81 Ky ., 375) ; and in an action on the policythe plaintiff'spetitionshould
show that he had such an interest. May on Insurance,? 590. It seems clear that A.'s
indebtednesstoB for$ 20.00does not make itlawful forB. to insureA.'s lifefor$ 2,000.00;
and thatRudwig's petitionwas defectivein failingto statethe amount of Gothelf's
indebtedness to him a defect, however , to which the attentionof the court does not
appear to have been calledby demurrer or otherwise.
(e) These bracketed words seem to be unnecessary. See note (b), ante, page 500.
(f ) The plaintiff's agreement to pay the premiums and the defendant's agreement to
pay the $2,000.00 being independentcovenants(see note 4 , ante, page 68), the averment of
payment of thepremiums would have been unnecessaryifthosepayments had notbeen,
as theywere, expresslydeclaredto be conditionsprecedenttotheplaintiff'srightof
recovery. See note (a), ante, page 500.
(g) This avermentwas unnecessary,the allegationof due noticeand proofof death
having been furnishedbeing sufficient: and, in an actionon a policyrequiring" due
noticeand proofofdeath," itisnotnecessaryfortheplaintiff'sprooftostatethefactsand
circumstances attending the death ; and if it unnecessarilystatethat the death resulted
from a causewhich,accordingtoa provisioninthepolicy,would make itvoid,an answer
of the defendant, averringthat the plaintiff'sproofstatedthe existenceof that fact,
would not presenta defence,the plaintiff'sallegationsconcerningnoticeand proof of
death not having been denied. ConnecticutMut . Life Ins. Co. v .Siegel, 9 Bush , 450 .
(h) Thisavermentwas unnecessary,and,standingalone,itwould havebeeninsufficient,
aspayment atany timebeforethe commencement of the actionwould have constituteda
defence .
(1) This avermentwas unnecessary,itbeing the defendant'sduty topay the money
withoutdemand ,withinsixtydaysafternoticeand proofofdeath; and,ifa demand had
been necessary, the mode of statingitwould have been insufficient. See note VI ,ante,
page 73 .
(j ) A prayer for intereston money " untilpaid" is not only unnecessary but seems
unadvisable; as,on a prayerforinterestomittingthosewords,thecourtmight givejudg-
ment forthe interestwhich had accrued, and the judgment thereforwould bear interest.
See G. S., ch.60 , 26 .

(c) Under chapter 81, 28 of the General Statutes, the Commonwealth must be the
obligeein allbonds forthe performance of any fiducialor public office; and , accordingto
the common law , only the Commonwealth has a legalrightof action thereon; but ch.48 ,
art. 1, 10, authorizesan actionon a guardian'sbond to be brought in the name of the
Commonwealth by the ward as relator; and ch .81,29 , declaresthat, for any breach of a
fiducialor officialbond , an actionin the name of the Commonwealth may be brought by
thepersoninjured,forhisbenefitand athiscosts.
But 18 of the Code declares that " every action must be prosecuted in the name of
the realparty in interest, except as isprovided in 221 ;" and 221 declares,interalia, that
" a person with whom or in whose name a contract ismade for the benefitof another
may bringan actionwithoutjoiningwith him the personforwhose benefititis
prosecuted;" and these provisionswere contained in the Codes of 1851 and 1854.
In Commonwealth for Harris v . Teal, & c., 14 В. М. , 29, itwas held that an action on
a constable'sbond , for an officialdefalcation, could be maintained in the name of the

1. In general, the assignee, in order to hold his assignorliable, must , with reasonable
diligence,bringan actionand obtaina judgment againstthe payor,inthecountyof his
residence,though he may have removed theretoaftertheassignment(8 B. M. ,132), and
deliver to the sheriffof that county a writ of fierifacias (2 Bibb, 35 ; 1 B. M. , 251 ; 6
Id.,418),and,also,a writof capiasad satisfaciendumifthe plaintiffbe entitledthereto
(see Code, 168; G. S.,ch.38,art.3 ; 1 Bibb, 546-47; 2 Id., 290; 2 Mar., 523); but he
need not appeal from an erroneousjudgment in favor of the payor. 3 Mon. , 63 .
What constitutesreasonablediligenceisfor the court,and not a jury,todecide(1
Bibb, 546; 1 Dana , 183; 80 Ky., 196); and an averment thattheplaintiffuseddue dili-
genceisinsufficientand unnecessary; he must statefactsshowingthathe used due dili-
gence (see note 2,ante, page 147, and 80 Ky ., 192); nor is there any general rule as to
what constitutesdue diligence,exceptthatthe assigneemust use suchdiligenceasa man
of ordinary prudence would be expected to use if the note were payable to himself (3
Bibb, 227 ; 6 Mon. , 652 ; 9 Dana , 46 ; 3 В. М. , 122, 587 ; 7 Id., 115; 8 Id., 229 ; 9 Id.,
55);- greaterdiligencebeing necessarywhen the payor isknown to be " in laboring cir-
cumstances " than when he isknown to be insolventor reputed to be solvent. 3 В. М. ,
587 ; 9 Id., 55.
A prima facie cause of action is stated by a petitionwhich shows that the assignee
obtainedjudgment againstthe payor, though by confession(9 Dana , 58), in the county of
hisresidence,at the firstterm of court afterthe note matured , or afteritwas assigned if
itwas then due ; and that a writ of fierifaciaswas issuedfrom the circuitcourt, though
not from a quarterlycourtorjustice'scourt(1 Met.,642),as soonasdue,and immediately
deliveredtothesheriffof thatcounty,and by him returned,on or beforethe return-day
(7 В. М. , 192)," no propertyfound;" and thata writof capiasad satisfaciendum, ifthe
plaintiffwas entitledthereto, was,withequaldiligence,deliveredtosaidsheriff,and that
thedefendant,having been arrestedthereon,was dischargedon takingtheoathof insol-
vency;-unless, aswillbe presentlyshown, itappearthattheplaintiffhad some other
remedy which he had failedto exhaust;-the returnof " no propertyfound" being,at
least,primafacieevidenceof the defendant'sinsolvency,and being,itseems,conclusive
evidencethereof,unlessitbe provedthattheplaintiffhad knowledge,actualor presump-
tive,of otherpropertyof the defendantwhich might have been subjected(2 Bibb,35; 4
J.J. M. ,304; 3 B. M. , 122; 6 Id.,418; 7 Id., 116); and the oath of insolvencybeing
entitledtothesame effectas,and,indeed,dispensingwiththenecessityof, thereturnof
no propertyfound. 5 Mon.,275-6.
But such extreme diligenceisnot usually, ifever, necessary.
(1) As todiligencein commencing suitagainstthepayor.
Assigneesfailingtoget judgment atthe firstterm aftertheyhad a rightto suehave,
in the absenceof explanatorycircumstances,been held to be inexcusablynegligentin
failingto sue during the followingperiodspreceding such term ,viz., four months (1 Mar. ,
230), threemonths (4 Mon. 15; 3 Dana ,596), thirty-threedays (8 B. M. , 229), sixteen
days (2 J. J. M. ,217); and delayof twenty-fourdays in suingbeforea justiceof the
peacewas held tobe inexcusable(3 Dana ,448); and the payorhavingbeen summoned in
timeforjudgment atthe firstterm,a continuanceof the case" by agreement" was held
tohave releasedthe assignorfrom liability(7 B. M. , 191-92): whilst,inthe absenceof
explanatorycircumstances,failureto sue fortwenty-two days,in one case(9 Dana ,45),
and for" more than fortydays," in another(8 B. M. , 299),was held to be excusable;
and,thepayorbeingtemporarilyabsentfrom theState,failuretosueforseventy-fivedays,
inone case(2 Litt., 132),and foreightydays,in another(6 B. M. ,418),was held to be
excusable; and delayattherequestof theassignorisexcusable. 3 Dana ,448.
(2) As todiligencein executingjudgment againstthepayor.
According to McMurray v .Woods ,9 Dana , 47, greater diligence is necessary in en-
forcingjudgmentagainstthe payorthanin obtainingit. Though itisnot necessaryfor
theplaintiffto applyforthe issuingof an executionbeforeit isregularlydue (2 Litt.,

fivedays. 3 В. М. ,584 .

example, Winnegar'sadm'r v.CentralPassengerRailwayCo., 85 Ky ., 547); and, in such
action, the contractisadmissiblein evidence as matter of inducement . 4 Bibb, 360.3. In an actionforfraud, itseems that exemplary damages ought to be recoverable, as

278, and Jackson'sex'rsv .Holliday'sadm'rs, 3 Mon. , 368 ; and opinions in Fleetand Sem-
ple v .Hollenkamp , 13 B. M. , 225, and Parker v .Jenkins, 3 Bush ,590 ; and see Hord v .

forthe deliveryof propertyat an agreed price; though itseems to be settledthat a fraudu-
lentvendor of land standson a worse footingthan an honest one who isunable to convey
title: see 3d paragraph of note (1), ante,page 77.

(a) 1. A fortiori, factswhich entitlea purchaser of land to rescissionof an executed
contract(seeForms 58,a,and 58,6) entitlehim torescissionof an executorycontract.
2.But fraudisnot necessarytoentitlea purchasertorescissionof an executorycon-
tract,the puchaser'sinabilityto convey titleaccording to his contract,and sometimes par-
tialinabilityto do so, being sufficientfor that purpose : see casescitedin notes to the next
followingForm .
3. Nor ,perhaps,isa purchaserwho obtainsa rescissionentitledto greaterreliefby
reasonof the vendor'sfraud than by reason of his mere inabilityto convey title: see note
(p), ante, p .582 ; to which I add that I have not found any decisionwhich holds that a
purchaser seeking rescissionof an executory contract for land is entitledto more than
reliefagainstunpaid purchase-money and the recoveryof purchase-money paid, with
interest,and of thevalueof improvementsput by him on theland.
4. But itseems clearthat, if such purchaser has been defrauded, he can maintain an
action for rescissionwithout bringing conflictingclaimants before the court, though he
may have been placedinpossessionof theland: seenote(a) 2, toForm 58,a ; Gillv.Cor.
bin,4 J.J.M. ,392; and Bullockv.Beemis, 1 Mar.,433,inwhich thevendor'snegligence
in adjustingconflictingclaimswhich were known tothe vendee atthe timeof his pur-
chasewas heldtobe " equivalenttofraud."
5. But if,notwithstandingthe fraud, thepurchaserdoes not claima rescissionabso-
lutely,but insistson a specificexecutionof the contractif titlecan be had , he must bring
conflictingclaimants before the court. Yoder v .Swearingen, 6 J. J. M. , 519-20.
6. If, however , there has been no fraud and he has had undisturbed possessionof the
land,he " can notbe entitledtoa rescissionwithoutan effortto procurethetitleorwith-
out showing that a good one can not be made ;" and it is his duty to bring conflicting
claimantsbeforethe court. Duvall v .Parker, 2 Duv ., 182 ; Casey, & c., v . Lucas,2 Bush ,
55: but see dictum in Prewitt v . Graves, 5 J. J. M. , 126.
7. In Hancock v .Byrne , 5 Dana , 514, which was an actionto rescindan executed con-
tract, the court saidthat, in a purchaser'saction to rescind an executory contract, the
burden lieson the defendant to show title; which is no doubt true as to casesof fraud;
and as to cases in which the purchaser has not received possessionand in which , after
making the necessaryallegations(see note (d ) to Form 59,b ), he callson the vendor for
an exhibitionof title(see Vittitoe& Enloe v .Jones & Triplett, 6 J. J. M. , 515); but ac-
cordingto Casey,& c.,v.Lucas,2 Bush,57,the burden of showing thatthe vendor has
no titleseems to reston the purchaser ifthere has been no fraud and he has had undis-
turbed possession.

(a) 1. GENERAL RULES AS TO DEFICITS AND SURPLUSES .
I have placeda form relatingtodeficitsand a form relatingto surplusesunder one
head, because the rulesare the same as to proving the existenceof a deficitor a surplus;
and as to showing whether or not itwas contemplatedby the parties; and as to de-
terminingwhether or not itisof such extent as to entitlea party to relief.
And , as to deficitsand surpluses,thereisno distinctionbetweenjudicialsalesand
privatesales(15 B. M. ,441; 4 Met., 106-7); nor isthereany distinctionbetween exe-
cuted and executory sales(2 Dana , 265 ; 9 B. M. , 176; 3 Met .,369); unlessit be true, as
was assertedin 2 Dana , 265, that, " in executorycontractsof sale in gross, itwillnot be
sodifficultto obtain relieffrom the literaleffectof the agreement as, forobvious reasons,
itmust generallybe afterthe legaltitlehas passed"- as to which see note(2), post,
p . 591 .
And no reliefwillbe givento a vendee fora deficit, nor to a vendorfora surplus,
which isprovedormust be presumedtohavebeencontemplatedby theparties,asisshown by
thecasescitedinthisparagraph; allofwhich relatetosalesingross,and intwo ofwhich,
namely ,Brown v . Parish, 2 Dana ,6, and Yancey v .Green,6 Id.,444, (and see Clark v. Bell,
4 ld., 15) the contractsfailedtostatethe quantityof land sold,and itwas heldthatthe
partiesmust be presumedtohave contemplatedany deficitorsurplusfoundtoexist; and
thatpresumption,ifnotconclusive,asitwouldseem tobe accordingtoYanceyv.Green,can
not,at any rate,be rebuttedexceptby showing a case" characterizedby peculiarcircum-
stancesof extraordinarymistake and indubitablehardship" (2 Dana , 9 ): and in two of
which the saleswere of stated quantities, " more or less; " namely , Young v .Craig, 2
Bibb,270,therebeing a surplusof 56 acresina tractsoldas 425 acres; and Pollackv.
Wilson, 3 Dana , 25, therebeing a deficitof 20 acresin a tractsold as 80 acres,a mill-seat,
however,which was not lostby reasonof the deficit, havingbeen theprincipalinduce-
ment to the purchase: and in the restof which casesthe words more or less" were not
used in the contracts; namely , Fisherv . May's heirs, 2 Bibb,448, there being a surplusof
22 acres in a tractsold as 400 acres; Clevelandv .Rodgers,& c., I Mar. , 193 (forcomments
onwhichsee2Dana,263),therebeinga surplusof 161acresina tractsoldas442acres;Elliot'sheirsv.Whaley, I Mar.,618,therebeing a surplusof 30 acresin a tractsoldas
185 acres; Rogers v .Garnett,4 Mon. , 269 (for comments on which see 2 Dana , 264), there
beinga surplusof 283 acresin a tractsoldas 2,000acres; Hampton'sheirsv.Eubank,4
J.J. M. ,634, therebeinga surplusof 35 acresina tractsoldas400 acres; and Eubank
v.Hampton, I Dana ,343, inwhich therewas surplusof 2 acresand a fractionina tract
soldas 51 acres,and inwhich fivepercent.was saidto be the customaryallowancefor
contingencies.
And , if the contract be executory, the vendee can compel a conveyance of the land,
includingthe surplus, without compensation for the surplus. Hampton's heirsv.Eubank ,
Eubank v .Hampton , and Clevelandv .Rogers, supra.
The chiefdistinctionbetween deficitsand surplusesisthatthe latternever do , whilst
the former may , authorizea rescissionof the contracton the ground of mistake.
2. DISTINCTION , AS TO DEFICITS AND SURPLUSES , BETWEEN SALES BY THE ACRE
AND SALES IN GROSS .
In Young v.Craig,2 Bibb,270,thecourt,aftersayingthatsalesoflandaresalesby the
acreor sales in gross, said: " It is evident that, in a saleper acre, much lessvariation
from the quantityintendedto be conveyedwould affordevidenceof a mistakewhich
would justifythe interpositionof a court to correctit, than would be sufficientfor that
purpose in a saleof the other description. It would be obviouslyimproper and
unjusttolaydown any generalruleastotherateof surplus[ordeficit] thatwouldjustify
an inferenceof a mistake which would deserve correction." And , in Harrison v . Talbott,
2 Dana , 266,the court saidwith referenceto salesin gross: " The relativeextent of the
surplus or deficitcan not,per se, furnish an infalliblecriterion. The conduct of the
parties- the dateof the contract[thatis,whetheritwas made at an earlyperiodof this

588 FORMS .
country, or not ; see 2 Bibb, 271-72]-the value and localityand extent of the land- the
price- andothernamelesscircumstancesarealwaysimportantandgenerallydecisive."
A contest between Elliott'sheirs and Whaley , concerning a surplus of 30 acres in a
tractsoldascontaining185acres,strikinglyillustratesthe distinctionbetween salesby
the acre and salesin gross, and alsoa ruleof constructionwith referencethereto: on the
firsthearing the court held that the land had been soldby the acre and that Elliott'sheirs
were entitledto the surplus(I Mar. ,343); but,on a re-hearing, the courtheld,on the same
facts,thatthelandhad been soldingross,and thatElliott'sheirswere notentitledto the
surplus . 1 Mar. , 618 .
It has sometimes been held that the words " more or less," followinga statementof
thequantityof landsold,showed thatthepartiescontemplateda deficitorsurplus(see2
Bibb,272): but,as isshown by caseshereinaftercited,thatpresumptionisnotnow con-
clusiveeven as to salesin gross, and does not ariseat all,perhaps, as to salesby the acre.
For severalgeneral rules concerning sales in gross see Harrison v . Talbott, 2 Dana ,
258, and casescited; Clark v .Bell, 4 Id., 15 ; Lewis v .Merriwether, 9 B. M. , 176, and
Fallv .Mc Murdy , 3 Met .,364.
(1) Casesrelatingtouncontemplateddeficitsana surpiuseson salesby theacre.
Vendees have been held to be entitledto relieffor deficits, or vendors to relieffor
surpluses,on salesby the acre of statedquantities, withoutsaying" more or less," in
the followingcases: Smith v. Smith, 4 Bibb, 81, as to a surplusof 24 acres in a tractsold
as containing 165 acres; Whaley v . Elliott'sheirs, 1 Mar. , 343, as to a surplusof 30 acres
in a tractsold as 185 acres; Buck & Conn v .McCaughtry , 5 Mon. , 216, 231, as to a deficit
of 50 acres on a tractsold as 787 acres; Grant v .Combs, 6 Mon. , 280, as to a deficitof
1032acresin a tractsold(at 121½ centsper acre)as containing2245 acres; Campbell's
